metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving step for AC3: either (a) a CI job that runs the reconciler against a fixture with the signing key env var set and asserts the written event carries `author_sig` (exercising the full wiring), or (b) an `[operator-attested]` tag with a recorded GHA run URL showing a signed event in the reconcile-bridge job logs, and remove or reframe 'the key is never logged' as a structural code-review assertion rather than an in-session verifiable criterion.
findings.finding: AC3 (workflow wiring) has no concrete proving command — no described test, CI step, or reconciler dry-run verifies that the secret injection, `::add-mask::`, `chmod 600`, and email-fallback change actually produce a signed event in the GHA environment; `make test` (AC4) exercises only the Python unit-test path and does not run the workflow.
findings.location: AC3; Workflow wiring section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and update BOTH occurrences of the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` fallback in `reconcile-bridge.yml` (line ~196 in 'Configure git identity' AND line ~235 in 'Commit reconciler events back and push') to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`.
findings.finding: The plan says to change 'that fallback default' in the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` env var (singular), but `reconcile-bridge.yml` contains TWO separate steps that each independently set the `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` default: the 'Configure git identity' step (line 196) AND the 'Commit reconciler events back and push' step (line 235). The plan's AC3 does not acknowledge both occurrences. If only the first is changed, git commits made in the second step will still carry the old noreply email as the committer, meaning `resolve_current_identity` won't map those commits to the bot identity.
findings.location: AC3 / .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml lines 196 and 235
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, replace 'CHANGE that fallback default' with 'CHANGE that fallback default in BOTH the "Configure git identity" step and the "Commit reconciler events back and push" step (two separate `env:` blocks in `reconcile-bridge.yml`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update BOTH occurrences of the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` fallback in `reconcile-bridge.yml` — line 196 ('Configure git identity') AND line 235 ('Commit reconciler events back and push') — to use `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` as the default.
findings.finding: `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` fallback appears in TWO distinct steps within `reconcile-bridge.yml` — line 196 ('Configure git identity for the bridge bot') AND line 235 ('Commit reconciler events back and push to origin/tickets') — but the plan only names the first step. If only the first occurrence is updated, the commit-back step still uses the old `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` fallback, which would fail to resolve to the bot identity when `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` is unset.
findings.location: AC line 3 / Workflow wiring section / .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml lines 196 and 235
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's AC 3 and Workflow wiring section, explicitly call out that both `env:` blocks in `reconcile-bridge.yml` that define `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` need the fallback changed: the 'Configure git identity' step (line 196) and the 'Commit reconciler events back and push' step (line 235).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Also change the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` fallback default in the 'Commit reconciler events back' step (reconcile-bridge.yml line 235) to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` so the git commit author on the tickets branch matches the `author_email` stamped in signed event envelopes.
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 specifies changing the fallback email only in the 'Configure git identity' step (line 196 of reconcile-bridge.yml), but the 'Commit reconciler events back' step (line 235) has an independent `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL: ${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com' }}` env entry that still uses the old fallback and feeds `git config user.email` in the tickets worktree. This creates an observable inconsistency: reconciler-authored events will carry `author_email: joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` in their signed envelope, but git commits on the tickets branch will record `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` as the committer when `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` is unset.
findings.location: AC3 ('CHANGE that fallback default to joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com'); reconcile-bridge.yml lines 234-240 ('Commit reconciler events back' step)
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 235 of reconcile-bridge.yml from `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL: ${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com' }}` to `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL: ${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com' }}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a safe `repo_root` derivation that handles the `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` override: prefer `config.repo_root()` (which reads from the canonical config path regardless of tracker location) over `tracker_dir.parent`, or explicitly guard against the override by checking `os.environ.get('REBAR_TRACKER_DIR')` and falling back to `config.repo_root()` when it is set.
findings.finding: The plan's proposed `repo_root` derivation (`tracker_dir.parent`) is incorrect when the `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` environment override is active. `_resolve_tracker_dir` returns an arbitrary absolute path when that override is set, making `tracker_dir.parent` an unrelated directory — causing `attribution_fields(repo_root)` to read the wrong git-email and `config.load_config(repo_root)` to load the wrong config, so identity resolution silently misses and events are written unsigned despite the signing key being configured.
findings.location: Chosen approach section: '_write_event_file will need repo_root available (derive from tracker_dir.parent or config.repo_root())'
findings.suggested_fix: In `_write_event_file`, derive repo_root as: `repo_root = Path(os.environ.get('REBAR_ROOT') or config.repo_root())` — mirroring the pattern already used in `_resolve_tracker_dir` and `apply_inbound.py:100-101`. This is safe whether or not `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` is active.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path for the fallback-email change — e.g., document that reverting AC3's fallback to `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` is the back-out step, OR use `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` (with no hardcoded fallback) so operators control the address without a code change.
findings.finding: The fallback-email change (from `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`) is an explicit behavioral change affecting all deployments that haven't set `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL`, but the plan provides no rollback/back-out path if the bot identity becomes unavailable or the email mapping breaks.
findings.location: AC3 / 'Workflow wiring' section — fallback default change
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, add a sentence: 'Back-out: if bot identity resolution fails, set `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL=rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` (repo variable) to revert to the prior committer address without a code change — the `vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` override takes precedence.' This makes the escape hatch explicit without requiring a code revert.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md note (or at minimum a prominent code comment block in `_write_event_file`) documenting WHY seam privates are called inline rather than routing through `append_event`, and acknowledge the coupling to `_seam._apply_authorship`/`attribution_fields` as an intentional, stable interface point.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — calling private seam helpers (`_seam._apply_authorship`, `_seam.attribution_fields`) directly from the reconciler, bypassing the seam's public API (`append_event`) — but does not capture this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or inline design doc. Future maintainers encountering the bypass have no in-repo explanation and may 'fix' it by routing through `append_event`, silently breaking the batch-commit model.
findings.location: Chosen approach section / Rationale paragraph; AC lines 1–2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to `CLAUDE.md` (or a new `docs/adr/0NNN-reconciler-inline-signing.md`) capturing: (1) the batch-commit invariant that prevents using `append_event`, (2) the two seam helpers used and their expected stability, and (3) how to update if the seam API changes. Also add a 3–5 line comment block above the new stamping code in `_write_event_file` cross-referencing that document.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify idempotency behavior for re-runs: either (a) skip re-signing if `author_sig` already present in the on-disk file, or (b) document that signatures are deterministic/stable across re-runs for the same event content, and add a test asserting this.
findings.finding: The plan does not address idempotency of `_write_event_file` under reconciler re-runs: if the GHA workflow retries (e.g., transient failure mid-batch), the same event files are re-written with freshly-computed DSSE signatures. Two workflow runs can produce different `author_sig` values for logically-identical events, and there is no dedup or stable-signature guarantee.
findings.location: Plan section 'Chosen approach: stamp + sign INLINE'; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Before stamping, check `if 'author_sig' not in event:` (reading from the existing file if present) and skip `_apply_authorship` if a signature already exists. Document this guard in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1) requiring a unit test that exercises the best-effort degradation path: when the signing key is absent or identity resolution fails, `_write_event_file` still writes the event (without `author_sig`) and does NOT raise an exception — asserting the reconciler's resilience to signing misconfigurations.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly describes a best-effort degradation path ('a miss writes unsigned') when `author_id` or `identity.signing_key` is absent, but no AC requires a test for this failure/fallback scenario — only the happy path (key present, identity resolves, signature verifies) is covered.
findings.location: AC1 (unit test) and 'Chosen approach' section: 'best-effort; a miss writes unsigned'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'A second parameterized case omits the signing key (or uses an unresolvable email) and asserts the event is still written with `author_email` present but no `author_sig`, and no exception is raised.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` fallback default in `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` (line ~100) from `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` to match the change planned for `reconcile-bridge.yml`.
findings.finding: The plan updates the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` fallback default only in `reconcile-bridge.yml` but the sibling workflow `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` contains the same `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL: ${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com' }}` fallback and is not mentioned. Any deployment using the canary workflow without the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` repo variable set will map reconciler writes to the old noreply address, leaving canary-path inbound events unsigned/unresolvable under the bot identity.
findings.location: AC3 / .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge-canary.yml line 100
findings.suggested_fix:
In `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge-canary.yml`, change:
  `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL: ${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com' }}`
to:
  `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL: ${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com' }}`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document the `_apply_authorship` error contract in the reconciler context: either (a) explicitly catch `CommandError` in the new `_write_event_file` signing block and log-and-continue (preserving the 'never breaks a write' invariant), or (b) note in the plan that `require_authenticated=True` intentionally gates reconciler writes too and that `CommandError` propagation is desired behavior — in either case, update AC line 1 to accurately characterize the behavior.
findings.finding: `_seam._apply_authorship` is a private function never called outside `_seam.py` today, and it contains a `raise CommandError(...)` code path (when `identity.require_authenticated=True` and the event cannot be signed for a non-exempt type). The reconciler's inbound layer has no import or handler for `CommandError`, so if this gate fires it will propagate as an unhandled exception through `_write_event_file` and fail the reconciler pass. The plan's 'best-effort; a miss writes unsigned' assertion is only accurate when `require_auth=False`; it is unverified for the `require_authenticated=True` case. No prior art exists in the codebase for calling `_apply_authorship` from outside `_seam.py`.
findings.location: Chosen approach section / AC line 1 ('best-effort; a miss writes unsigned') / `src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py:317-325`
findings.suggested_fix: In `_write_event_file`, wrap the `_seam._apply_authorship(...)` call in a broad `except Exception` block (mirroring the pattern already used for signing failures in `_apply_authorship` itself) and log a warning rather than propagating. This preserves the 'signing never breaks a write' contract regardless of the `require_authenticated` config state, and is consistent with the reconciler's tolerance-first error model.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `rm -f "$KEY_FILE"` cleanup step (with `if: always()`) to the workflow so the temp signing-key file is deleted whether the job succeeds or fails; or declare that only GitHub-hosted (ephemeral) runners are used and add a comment justifying why cleanup is unnecessary.
findings.finding: The plan specifies writing `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` to a `mktemp` temp file and masking its value with `::add-mask::`, but does not specify cleanup (deletion) of the temp file after use, nor does it state whether the workflow runs on GitHub-hosted (ephemeral) or self-hosted (persistent) runners. On self-hosted runners, the chmod-600 temp file containing the signing key would persist on disk between workflow runs, violating least-privilege / secrets hygiene.
findings.location: AC line 3 / `reconcile-bridge.yml` workflow wiring section
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a final workflow step:
```yaml
- name: Clean up signing key
  if: always()
  run: rm -f "$REBAR_BOT_KEY_FILE"
```
where `REBAR_BOT_KEY_FILE` is the env var set to the `mktemp` path. Alternatively, scope the key-file creation to a single shell step using a heredoc + process substitution so the file never hits disk.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify idempotency behavior for `_write_event_file` when the target file already exists and already carries `author_sig`: either (a) skip re-stamping if the file is already signed, or (b) document that overwriting with a fresh signature on retry is acceptable and why (e.g., reconciler passes are always full-corpus, not incremental).
findings.finding: The inline stamping in `_write_event_file` is not described as idempotent. If the reconciler retries or re-runs for the same event (e.g., due to a transient failure mid-pass), `_write_event_file` may be called again for an already-written file. The plan does not specify whether the function overwrites, skips, or errors on a pre-existing signed file, creating a resumability gap.
findings.location: AC line 1 — '`_write_event_file` stamps … inline before writing'; Plan body — '_write_event_file intentionally writes an uncommitted file'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit cleanup of the temp signing-key file immediately after the reconciler step (e.g., `trap 'rm -f "$KEY_FILE"' EXIT` in the step that creates the temp file, or a dedicated cleanup step that runs with `if: always()`), so the plaintext key does not persist for the job's remaining steps.
findings.finding: The plan specifies writing secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY to a mktemp file and masking the value, but never specifies deletion of the temp file after it is no longer needed. The plaintext private SSH key persists on the runner filesystem for the entire remaining job (push retries, notify step, re-dispatch step), widening the exposure window beyond the signing step itself.
findings.location: Workflow wiring section (AC line 3): 'write secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY to a job-local file (mktemp, chmod 600, ::add-mask:: the value)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the step that creates the temp key file, wrap it with a trap: `KEY_FILE=$(mktemp); trap 'rm -f "$KEY_FILE"' EXIT; echo '::add-mask::$KEY_FILE'; chmod 600 "$KEY_FILE"; printf '%s' "${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}" > "$KEY_FILE"`. Alternatively, add a final `if: always()` step: `- name: Clean up signing key\n  if: always()\n  run: rm -f "$SIGNING_KEY_PATH"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `identity.signing_key` vs `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` ambiguity in the workflow wiring section: state which variable (or both) must be set, and add a concrete example line to the AC3 workflow step.
findings.finding: The plan says to 'set `identity.signing_key` (or `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY`)' in the workflow, leaving the executor to choose between a config key and an env var alias — this is an unresolved branch that could produce a workflow that silently fails to inject the signing key.
findings.location: Workflow wiring section; AC3
findings.suggested_fix:
Pick one canonical mechanism (e.g. `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` env var) and state it unambiguously: `- name: Set signing key
  run: |
    KEY_FILE=$(mktemp)
    chmod 600 $KEY_FILE
    echo '${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}' > $KEY_FILE
    echo '::add-mask::${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}'
    echo "REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY=$KEY_FILE" >> $GITHUB_ENV`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `attribution_fields` is also called best-effort (wrap in try/except), so a resolution failure falls back to omitting author enrichment fields rather than aborting the inbound write.
findings.finding: `_seam.attribution_fields(repo_root)` is called without any specified error handling, yet only `_apply_authorship` is explicitly called out as best-effort. If `attribution_fields` raises (e.g., repo_root unresolvable, git config missing), the exception would propagate up through `_write_event_file` and abort the entire inbound batch write — a hard failure on every Jira-sourced event in that reconciler pass.
findings.location: Plan section 'Chosen approach: stamp + sign INLINE', step 1; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap step 1 in a try/except: `try: event.update(_seam.attribution_fields(repo_root)) except Exception: pass` (matching the best-effort contract already stated for step 2), and document this in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 1) requiring that signing misses emit at least a `logger.warning` (with ticket_id and event_type) so operators can observe and alert on unsigned-event writes.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for signing failures — when `_apply_authorship` misses (best-effort) or `attribution_fields` falls back, the event is written unsigned/unenriched with no log line, metric, or warning. Operators cannot distinguish a correctly-unsigned event from a silently-failed signing attempt without manually running `rebar verify-authorship`.
findings.location: Plan section 'Chosen approach: stamp + sign INLINE'; AC lines 1–2
findings.suggested_fix: In the best-effort except block for `_apply_authorship`, add: `logger.warning('rebar-bridge: signing miss for %s/%s — event written unsigned', ticket_id, event_type)`. Mirror the same for an `attribution_fields` fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle the plan (or add a clarifying subtitle) to reflect the actual mechanism: e.g. 'GHA reconcile-bridge: stamp + sign inbound writes inline via seam helpers + inject key' — so the title is not contradicted by the chosen approach section.
findings.finding: The plan title says 'route inbound writes THROUGH signing seam' but the plan body explicitly rejects routing through the seam's main entry point (`_seam.append_event`) and instead calls seam helpers inline. An implementer reading only the title could attempt to route through `append_event`, breaking the batch-commit model the plan specifically preserves.
findings.location: Title: 'GHA reconcile-bridge: route inbound writes through signing seam + inject key' vs. 'Chosen approach' section: 'stamp + sign INLINE (not via `_seam.append_event`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to: 'GHA reconcile-bridge: inline stamp + sign inbound writes via seam helpers + inject key' to accurately reflect that seam helpers are called inline rather than routing through `append_event`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `_write_event_file` API change: specify whether to add an optional `repo_root: Path | None = None` kwarg (derived as `tracker_dir.parent` when None) so callers that already have `repo_root` can pass it explicitly, and document that REBAR_TRACKER_DIR overrides in tests must be accompanied by the correct `repo_root` for signing resolution to work.
findings.finding: The plan says '_write_event_file will need repo_root available (derive from tracker_dir.parent or config.repo_root())' but leaves the API shape ambiguous: it does not specify whether to add an optional repo_root kwarg (defaulting to internal derivation) vs. purely internal derivation with no parameter change. Several callers already have repo_root in scope (e.g. apply_inbound_records._inbound_create_write_create_event, line 84) but the intermediate helpers they call (e.g. _inbound_create_write_status_event, which calls _write_event_file directly) do not receive repo_root. In test contexts that set REBAR_TRACKER_DIR to an arbitrary path, tracker_dir.parent would be the wrong repo_root, causing attribution_fields() to read git config from the wrong directory and potentially resolving to no identity.
findings.location: Plan section 'Chosen approach: stamp + sign INLINE'; AC1 ('_write_event_file will need repo_root available')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `repo_root: Path | None = None` as a keyword argument to `_write_event_file`; inside, resolve it as `repo_root = repo_root or tracker_dir.parent`. Update callers that already receive `repo_root` (e.g. `_inbound_create_write_create_event`) to pass it through; callers without it fall back to `tracker_dir.parent` automatically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema-state assertion to the plan: explicitly state that after reverting the signing code, the store contains a mix of signed and unsigned events, that unsigned-first readers silently ignore `author_sig`/`author_id`/`author_email` (cite the additive guarantee), and that no re-processing or data corruption results — confirming the mixed state is a valid, stable resting state.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the persisted-store state AFTER a code rollback. If the signing code is reverted, the store will contain a mix of signed (author_sig present) and unsigned events. The plan's additive-compatibility claim covers forward reads, but does not explicitly state that: (1) the mixed state is valid for all readers, and (2) signed events written before rollback are not corrupted or re-processed incorrectly by the reverted code.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Rationale' / 'The added author fields are additive (older readers ignore unknown keys — no store-format break)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback safety' subsection: 'If this change is reverted, existing signed events remain in the store with their extra fields intact. The reverted code ignores unknown keys (additive guarantee), so the mixed corpus is a valid stable state. No compensating migration is required. Events signed before rollback retain their signatures but are no longer actively verified by new writes — this is acceptable.'

findings.finding: The plan's AC#3 says the `.yml` file 'sets `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`', but in the existing `reconcile-bridge.yml` this value is read from a GitHub repo variable (`${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com' }}`). Changing the email requires updating the GitHub repo variable — an operational step outside the workflow file — which no AC or edit target enumerates.
findings.location: AC#3 — 'sets `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`'; reconcile-bridge.yml lines 196, 235

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
